August 2021

What to do with data?

  • You have your clean data, now what?
  • You need to understand how variables move together!
  • Two main tools:
    • Correlation analysis.
    • Regression analysis.

Motivation

  • In this lecture, we are going to have a look at S&P stock returns.
  • We select the 10 biggest capitalization.
    • Apple (AAPL)
    • Microsoft (MSFT)
    • Amazon (AMZN)
    • Facebook (FB)
    • Alphabet Inc. A shares (GOOGL)
    • Alphabet Inc. C shares (GOOG)
    • Berkshire Hathaway (BRK-B)
    • JP Morgan (JPM)
    • Tesla (TSLA)
    • Johnson & Johnson (JNJ)

Portfolio optimization with stocks

  • You may know that two principles are sacred in portfolio optimization:
    • Diversification.
    • the more asset returns co-move, the less you want both of them.
  • More formally, Markowitz portfolio (see investment class) tells you that the optimal asset allocation depends on the inverse covariance matrix of asset returns.
  • We will explore this idea with our 10 stocks.

Constructing stock returns

Stock returns analysis

  • Let us look at the S&P500 stock data during 2020.
  • We download stock data directly from our yahoo finance API.
  • we have 20 years of daily data, from 2000 to 2020.
import yfinance as yf

assets = ['AAPL', 'MSFT', 'AMZN', 'FB', 'GOOGL', 
          'GOOG', 'BRK-B', 'JPM', 'TSLA', 'JNJ']
prices =  yf.download(assets,
                      start='2000-01-01',
                      end='2020-12-31',
                      progress=False)

prices.shape
## (5283, 60)
prices.columns.droplevel(1).drop_duplicates()
## Index(['Adj Close', 'Close', 'High', 'Low', 'Open', 'Volume'], dtype='object')

Stock returns analysis

  • We need to create the returns data.
  • Let’s drop every variable but the close first.
variable_names = prices.columns.droplevel(1).drop_duplicates().to_list()
variable_names.remove('Close')
print(variable_names)
## ['Adj Close', 'High', 'Low', 'Open', 'Volume']
for asset_name in assets:
  for variable in variable_names:
    column_name = (variable, asset_name)
    del prices[column_name]

Stock returns analysis

print(prices)
##                  Close               ...                        
##                   AAPL         AMZN  ...        MSFT        TSLA

Some plot

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
prices[('Close', 'AAPL')].plot();
plt.xlabel("Date"); plt.ylabel("Close"); plt.title("AAPL");
plt.show()

Some plots

  • With a multiple layout and matplotlib
fig = plt.figure();
fig, axs = plt.subplots(5, 2);
count = 0
for i in range(0,5):
  for j in range(0,2):
    axs[i,j].plot(prices['Close'][assets[count]]);
    axs[i,j].set_title(assets[count]);
    count = count + 1

Some plots

Creating returns

  • Now we need to transform these prices into returns.
  • Fortunately, there is a function already done for us!
  • We use .pct_change
returns = prices.pct_change()
returns = returns.droplevel(0, axis = 1)
returns
##                 AAPL      AMZN     BRK-B  ...       JPM      MSFT      TSLA
## Date                                      ...                              
## 2000-01-03       NaN       NaN       NaN  ...       NaN       NaN       NaN
## 2000-01-04 -0.084310 -0.083217 -0.034561  ... -0.027444 -0.033780       NaN
## 2000-01-05  0.014633 -0.148741  0.016432  ... -0.006173  0.010544       NaN
## 2000-01-06 -0.086538 -0.060036  0.041571  ...  0.014197 -0.033498       NaN
## 2000-01-07  0.047369  0.061010  0.008869  ...  0.018373  0.013068       NaN
## ...              ...       ...       ...  ...       ...       ...       ...
## 2020-12-23 -0.006976 -0.006627  0.010636  ...  0.027944 -0.013039  0.008808
## 2020-12-24  0.007712 -0.003949  0.010212  ... -0.004398  0.007827  0.024444

Plotting returns

import plotnine as plt9

returns_plot = returns.stack().reset_index()
returns_plot.columns = ["Date", "stock", "return"]
(
  plt9.ggplot(returns_plot)
    + plt9.aes(x = "Date", y = "return", colour = "stock")
    + plt9.facet_wrap('stock')
    + plt9.geom_line()
    + plt9.theme(axis_text_x=plt9.element_text(angle=45),
                 strip_text_y=plt9.element_text(size=5), 
                 subplots_adjust={'right': 0.8})
    + plt9.labs(title = "Stock returns", y = "Natural daily units", x = "")
)

Plotting returns

## <ggplot: (-9223372036748728190)>

Covariance Analysis

Statistical moments to analyze co-movement

  • Covariance and correlations are the main objects of interest to measure how variables move together.
  • The covariance between \(X\) and \(Y\) is given by: \[ Cov(X,Y) = E(XY) - E(X)E(Y)\,. \]
  • for vectors, the covariance is a matrix where entry \((i,j)\) is the covariance of \(X_i\) and \(X_j\).
  • In practice, computation is performed with: \[ \widehat{Cov}(X,Y) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^T \left(x_{t} - \overline{x}\right)\left(y_{t}-\overline{y}\right)\,. \]
  • Standard deviation is given by: \[ \widehat{\sigma}_X = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T-1}\sum_{t=1}^T \left(x_{t} - \overline{x}\right)^2} \]

Moments to analyze co-movement

  • Combining both, we can obtain Pearson Correlation: \[ \widehat{\rho}_{X,Y} = \frac{\widehat{Cov}(X,Y) }{\widehat{\sigma}_X \cdot \widehat{\sigma}_Y} \]
  • \(\widehat{\rho}_{X,Y} \in [-1,1]\).
  • If \(\widehat{\rho}_{X,Y} =0\), the variables are uncorrelated (they don’t co-move).
  • If \(\widehat{\rho}_{X,Y} =\pm 1\), the variables are perfectly correlated, hence redundant.
  • Other less popular correlation measures exist, Spearman’s and Kendall’s rank correlations.

Calling correlations in Python

  • You can compute covariance and correlations between columns directly with pandas.
import numpy as np
round(100*np.sqrt(returns.cov()),2)
##        AAPL  AMZN  BRK-B    FB  GOOG  GOOGL   JNJ   JPM  MSFT  TSLA
## AAPL   2.56  1.72   0.95  1.30  1.43   1.42  0.85  1.52  1.52  1.38
## AMZN   1.72  3.25   1.04  1.43  1.51   1.51  0.80  1.61  1.63  1.49
## BRK-B  0.95  1.04   1.44  0.98  1.04   1.04  0.78  1.33  0.99  1.09
## FB     1.30  1.43   0.98  2.36  1.37   1.37  0.82  1.09  1.27  1.46
## GOOG   1.43  1.51   1.04  1.37  1.93   1.92  0.89  1.41  1.32  1.33
## GOOGL  1.42  1.51   1.04  1.37  1.92   1.92  0.89  1.41  1.32  1.33
## JNJ    0.85  0.80   0.78  0.82  0.89   0.89  1.24  1.01  0.92  0.83
## JPM    1.52  1.61   1.33  1.09  1.41   1.41  1.01  2.48  1.49  1.28
## MSFT   1.52  1.63   0.99  1.27  1.32   1.32  0.92  1.49  1.95  1.35
## TSLA   1.38  1.49   1.09  1.46  1.33   1.33  0.83  1.28  1.35  3.55

Calling correlations in Python

  • You can compute covariance and correlations between columns directly with pandas.
returns.corr().round(2)
##        AAPL  AMZN  BRK-B    FB  GOOG  GOOGL   JNJ   JPM  MSFT  TSLA
## AAPL   1.00  0.35   0.24  0.39  0.50   0.50  0.23  0.36  0.46  0.30
## AMZN   0.35  1.00   0.23  0.45  0.49   0.49  0.16  0.32  0.42  0.31
## BRK-B  0.24  0.23   1.00  0.34  0.41   0.41  0.34  0.49  0.35  0.27
## FB     0.39  0.45   0.34  1.00  0.50   0.50  0.26  0.30  0.42  0.26
## GOOG   0.50  0.49   0.41  0.50  1.00   1.00  0.37  0.43  0.53  0.30
## GOOGL  0.50  0.49   0.41  0.50  1.00   1.00  0.38  0.43  0.53  0.30
## JNJ    0.23  0.16   0.34  0.26  0.37   0.38  1.00  0.33  0.35  0.18
## JPM    0.36  0.32   0.49  0.30  0.43   0.43  0.33  1.00  0.46  0.25
## MSFT   0.46  0.42   0.35  0.42  0.53   0.53  0.35  0.46  1.00  0.32
## TSLA   0.30  0.31   0.27  0.26  0.30   0.30  0.18  0.25  0.32  1.00
  • This is already very informative!

Rank Correlations in Python

  • You can also compute rank correlations
returns.corr(method = 'spearman').round(2)
##        AAPL  AMZN  BRK-B    FB  GOOG  GOOGL   JNJ   JPM  MSFT  TSLA
## AAPL   1.00  0.41   0.26  0.41  0.48   0.48  0.23  0.37  0.48  0.31
## AMZN   0.41  1.00   0.28  0.53  0.55   0.55  0.23  0.35  0.46  0.35
## BRK-B  0.26  0.28   1.00  0.32  0.39   0.39  0.31  0.45  0.31  0.26
## FB     0.41  0.53   0.32  1.00  0.57   0.58  0.23  0.31  0.44  0.31
## GOOG   0.48  0.55   0.39  0.57  1.00   1.00  0.33  0.42  0.50  0.33
## GOOGL  0.48  0.55   0.39  0.58  1.00   1.00  0.33  0.42  0.50  0.33
## JNJ    0.23  0.23   0.31  0.23  0.33   0.33  1.00  0.33  0.33  0.18
## JPM    0.37  0.35   0.45  0.31  0.42   0.42  0.33  1.00  0.43  0.24
## MSFT   0.48  0.46   0.31  0.44  0.50   0.50  0.33  0.43  1.00  0.31
## TSLA   0.31  0.35   0.26  0.31  0.33   0.33  0.18  0.24  0.31  1.00

Rank Correlations in Python

  • You can also compute rank correlations
returns.corr(method = 'kendall').round(2)
##        AAPL  AMZN  BRK-B    FB  GOOG  GOOGL   JNJ   JPM  MSFT  TSLA
## AAPL   1.00  0.29   0.18  0.29  0.35   0.35  0.16  0.26  0.34  0.21
## AMZN   0.29  1.00   0.20  0.39  0.40   0.40  0.16  0.25  0.33  0.24
## BRK-B  0.18  0.20   1.00  0.22  0.27   0.27  0.22  0.33  0.22  0.18
## FB     0.29  0.39   0.22  1.00  0.42   0.43  0.16  0.21  0.31  0.21
## GOOG   0.35  0.40   0.27  0.42  1.00   0.96  0.23  0.30  0.36  0.22
## GOOGL  0.35  0.40   0.27  0.43  0.96   1.00  0.23  0.29  0.36  0.22
## JNJ    0.16  0.16   0.22  0.16  0.23   0.23  1.00  0.23  0.23  0.12
## JPM    0.26  0.25   0.33  0.21  0.30   0.29  0.23  1.00  0.31  0.16
## MSFT   0.34  0.33   0.22  0.31  0.36   0.36  0.23  0.31  1.00  0.21
## TSLA   0.21  0.24   0.18  0.21  0.22   0.22  0.12  0.16  0.21  1.00
  • Be careful! All these correlations are computed by throwing away the rows with NA.
  • Even if GOOGL and MSFT are observable for longer periods than FB, computations are performed for data observed only when FB is observable.
  • You need to perform a loop to obtain more flexible correlations.

Exercise

  1. Download a set of three stocks different from those used in this lecture using the yfinance library, from 1980 until 2020.
  2. Compute their correlation matrix using the built-in pandas .cov
  3. Use a loop to compute the pairwise correlation stock by stock. Compare.
  4. Using the scipy library, and the following piece of code, compare the eigenvalues of the correlation matrices. Are they all positive?
import scipy.linalg as la
# CORR_MAT is your correlation matrix
eigenvalues = la.eig(CORR_MAT)

Regression analysis

The linear regression framework

  • The other tool to check the relationship between variables is the regression.
plt.plot(returns['AAPL'], returns['MSFT'], '+'); plt.xlabel('AAPL'); plt.ylabel('MSFT');
plt.show();

The linear regression framework

  • The other tool to check the relationship between variables is the regression.
plt.plot(returns['AAPL'], returns['MSFT'], '+'); plt.xlabel('AAPL'); plt.ylabel('MSFT');
plt.show();

Performing regressions in Python

  • Let us use the example of the CAPM.
  • We will use a market portfolio \(R_t\) defined as the mean of our 10 returns.
  • We assume the riskless rate is zero.
  • The model writes: \[ r_{i,t} = \alpha_i + \beta_i \cdot R_t + \varepsilon_{i,t} \]
  • We can estimate this regression with the statsmodel.api library.
import statsmodels.api as sm
  • Other libraries also provide linear (and other) regression(s).
    • NumPy provides the polyfit function for polynomial regressions.
    • The Scikit-Learn library sklearn has the LinearRegression function.

Performing regressions in Python

  • Let us run the regression for AAPL.
# Computing the market return
Mkt = returns.mean(axis = 1)
Mkt.name = "Market"

# Adding the constant in the regression
regressors = sm.add_constant(Mkt)
regressors
##             const    Market
## Date                       
## 2000-01-03    1.0       NaN
## 2000-01-04    1.0 -0.049987
## 2000-01-05    1.0 -0.017125
## 2000-01-06    1.0 -0.015495
## 2000-01-07    1.0  0.031871
## ...           ...       ...
## 2020-12-23    1.0  0.002927
## 2020-12-24    1.0  0.004985
## 2020-12-28    1.0  0.018353
## 2020-12-29    1.0 -0.001293
## 2020-12-30    1.0 -0.001255
## 
## [5283 rows x 2 columns]

Performing regressions in Python

  • Let us run the regression for AAPL.
# Telling Python which model we are going to fit
Model = sm.OLS(returns['AAPL'], regressors, missing = 'drop')

# Fitting the model
Fit = Model.fit()
# print(Fit.summary())

Regression results

from statsmodels.iolib.summary2 import summary_col
# print(summary_col([Fit],stars=True))

Running regressions

Exercise

  1. Download the same 10 stocks data.
  2. Perform the CAPM regression for all stocks using a for loop, and store the results in a list or a dictionnary.
  3. What is the stock with the highest/lowest alpha?
  4. What is the stock with the highest/lowest beta?
  5. Looking aat the \(R^2\), what are the stock returns mostly correlated with the market?
  6. Looking at the 10 betas, are the stock returns positively or negatively correlated?

Presentations and reports with RMarkdown

Performing presentations and code

  • These slides have been performed with RMarkdown, a module of RStudio.

Rstudio

  • Presentation of RStudio is similar to Spyder, except it’s mainly done for R.
  • You can also use Python with Rstudio.
  • You just need to run the following piece of code:
install.packages("reticulate") # Installs the module to perform Python
# The installation is to be done only ONCE!
library(reticulate) # Calls the package

# Install Python modules
py_install("numpy")
py_install("pandas")
py_install("matplotlib")

# Starting Python
repl_python()
  • The console tells you Python starts:

The knitr package

  • The knitr package allows you to create Rmarkdown documents.
install.packages("knitr")
  • Then you can start with a Rmarkdown document.
  • You can create two types of documents:
    • Report
    • Slides
  • Both of them can be either HTML (like these slides) or PDF.

Creating Markdown docs

Creating Markdown docs

Creating Markdown docs

A new Markdown document

  • Markdown provides you with a template:

Compiling a Markdown document

  • You can find out how your presentation looks by clicking on knit

Anatomy of a Markdown document

  • Section/Slides Titles are provided with # and ##.
  • Text is freely included.
  • bullet points are indicated with -
  • Chunks of code begin with ``` and close with ```.
  • Chunks can indicate which language you are using, in our case, Python, followed by the name of the chunk.
  • We can include other options:
    • echo = T: print the source code verbatim. If FALSE, only the output is printed.
    • eval = F: do not evaluate the chunk.

```{python, echo = T}

import numpy as np

toto = np.array([])

```

Advantage of RMarkdown

  • This allows you to perform code and report at the same time.
  • This shows the code you have been using (for teaching purposes for instance).
  • This updates automatically your results when data is updated.
  • Documents can contain TeX instructions for typing mathematics!

BE CAREFUL: - When you knit the document, this re-evaluates all code chunks. - You may deactivate it chunk by chunk by using the chunk option cache = T.

Further markdown knowledge

  • The best resource for Markdown is a book by Yihui Xie, to be found for free here
  • There are many many many things you can do with Markdown, you’ll have to discover it on your own!
  • You can also use Jupyter if you are attached to sticking with the Python universe.
